project.title: Teens Trust AI More Than They Should
project.subtitle: A Study of Canadian High School Students' AI Risk Perception
project.mentor: Professor Tao Wang, Faculty of Information, University of Toronto

researchQuestions: How do teenagers encounter and use AI in daily life?
How do teenagers demonstrate understanding and critical awareness of AI systems?
How do parents and educators influence teenagers' attitudes toward and usage of AI?

userCategories.label: Frequent Users
userCategories.description: Use AI more than once a week

userCategories.label: Non-frequent Users
userCategories.description: Use AI once a week or less

comfortParadox.description: The more teens use AI, the less they believe risks outweigh benefits

doWorryAbout.label: Will AI make me a worse thinker?
Will my writing skills get worse?
Is the AI giving me wrong info?

dontWorryAbout.label: AI making unfair decisions about me

trustAIFeedback.description: I trust AI feedback as much as a teacher's

openToAIInLearning.description: I am open to using AI in learning

dataSharingAttitudes.quote: Like basically everything, like, you know, obviously not like my Social Security number, my like passport, not like those type of stuff, but like everything else, sure.
How much control do you feel you have over what personal data AI collects about you? 0%.

aiEmotionalSupport.quote: Using ChatGPT for therapy or coping mechanisms and vent thoughts also works.
People say don't do that but I don't see the harm in it.
It's more empathetic and you don't bother anyone.
ChatGPT will pretty much align with whatever you say. It is less prominent but still there.

aiAndGroupWork.quote: Some of my group mates have used AI to completely generate their parts of the project... resulting in inconsistent quality and tone.
People get flagged by AI detectors even for original work, and it creates stress and mistrust between students and teachers.
I think using AI to deepen understanding is a positive use. But using it as a complete substitute for original work is negative.
AI is replacing the brainstorming and discussion that used to happen in group work. We used to actually talk about ideas.

drawingBoundaries.quote: Never asks AI for emotional advice — only uses it for academic stuff.
Friends would always be better for personal matters.

conclusions: AI is deeply integrated into student life, especially for academic tasks, and usage increases with grade level and among female students.
Risk awareness exists but remains low overall, especially for privacy, cybersecurity, and emotional risks.
Frequent users show significantly lower concern across all risk categories, suggesting that habituated use reduces perceived risk even when vulnerabilities remain.
A minority of teens use AI for emotional support, viewing it as empathetic or nonjudgmental, creating potential psychological risks.
The findings reveal a widening gap between actual risks and students' perceived risks, underscoring the need for targeted education.

forSchools: Integrate AI literacy into curricula, including misinformation detection, ethical use, privacy basics, and AI limitations.
Provide clear, age-appropriate guidelines for academic use to reduce over-reliance and protect learning integrity.
Implement structured discussions about emotional boundaries with AI.
forParents: Encourage open dialogue about students' AI habits and discuss risk awareness, especially around privacy and emotional use.
Model responsible AI practices at home and align expectations with school guidelines.
forPolicymakers: Strengthen AI safety standards for youth, including data protection and transparency requirements.
Support teacher training to help educators guide students through safe and effective AI use.
Promote nationwide digital literacy initiatives that empower teens to critically evaluate AI outputs and manage risks.

forEducators.title: Teach WITH AI, Not Against It
forEducators.description: 57% of teens are open to AI in learning, but 71% worry it will harm their thinking. Design assignments that require students to critically evaluate AI outputs rather than banning AI entirely.

forEducators.title: Address the AI Detector Problem
forEducators.description: Students report anxiety about AI detectors flagging their original work. Consider process-based assessment (drafts, reflections, oral explanations) instead of relying solely on detection tools.

forEducators.title: Don't Assume Gender Stereotypes
forEducators.description: Girls use AI just as much or more than boys (60% vs 51% frequent users). AI literacy programming should be designed for all students, not stereotyped toward 'tech-oriented' youth.

forEducators.title: Focus on the Thinking, Not the Tool
forEducators.description: Students' top concern is losing their ability to think critically. Help them build metacognitive skills: when to use AI, when to struggle on their own, and how to recognize when AI is weakening their understanding.

forYouthWorkers.title: Fill the Parent Gap on AI Safety
forYouthWorkers.description: Only 43% of parents have discussed AI safety. Youth workers can provide the trusted, non-judgmental space where teens can ask questions about AI that they can't or won't ask at home or school.

forYouthWorkers.title: Address the Safety Blind Spot
forYouthWorkers.description: Teens rate online scams and grooming as their LOWEST AI concern (2.60/5). Create engaging workshops that demonstrate these risks concretely — not with lectures, but with live demos.

forYouthWorkers.title: Support the Emotional Dimension
forYouthWorkers.description: 57% of teens are concerned about emotional manipulation by AI. Youth workers should be prepared to discuss AI companionship, boundary-setting with chatbots, and recognizing when AI interactions become unhealthy.

forYouthWorkers.title: Reach Younger Teens Early
forYouthWorkers.description: Gr. 9-10 students use AI less frequently (37% frequent users) but are at a formative stage. This is the ideal window for building critical AI literacy BEFORE heavy usage begins.

keyTakeaways.title: AI is already mainstream for teens
keyTakeaways.description: 55% use it weekly for school — this is not a future trend, it's today's reality.

keyTakeaways.title: Teens worry about losing their ability to think
keyTakeaways.description: Performance risk, not privacy, is their top concern. They know AI could weaken them.

keyTakeaways.title: The more they use AI, the less they worry
keyTakeaways.description: Heavy users may be underestimating real risks. Familiarity breeds comfort, not mastery.

keyTakeaways.title: Social media is their AI teacher
keyTakeaways.description: Parents and schools are falling behind. Libraries and trusted institutions can fill this gap.

keyTakeaways.title: Teens want guidance, not gatekeeping
keyTakeaways.description: 57% are open to AI in learning but prefer teacher feedback. Help them use it well.
